questions.question: Explain the core concept of density-based clustering and how it differs from partitioning methods like K-Means.
keyPoints: Clusters are defined as areas of high point density separated by areas of low density.
Does not require pre-specifying the number of clusters (K).
Can find clusters of arbitrary, non-spherical shapes, unlike K-Means which typically finds spherical clusters.

questions.question: Describe the role of the two parameters, epsilon (ε) and MinPts, in the DBSCAN algorithm.
keyPoints: Epsilon (ε): The radius of the neighborhood around a point.
MinPts: The minimum number of points required within the ε-neighborhood to define a core point.
These parameters together determine the density threshold required to form a cluster.

questions.question: Compare and contrast DBSCAN with the OPTICS algorithm. Discuss a key limitation of DBSCAN that OPTICS aims to solve and explain how OPTICS addresses it.
keyPoints: Key DBSCAN limitation: Struggles with clusters of varying densities because it uses a single global ε parameter.
OPTICS solves this by creating a reachability plot, which is a bar chart that represents the density-based clustering structure of the data.
Instead of producing a single clustering, OPTICS outputs an ordering of the points and their core and reachability distances, allowing for extraction of clusters across a range of parameter settings.
